# Progress and challenges in self-healing composite materials

Shafiqul
Islam
[![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0001-7834-4100 "Select to open ORCID record for Shafiqul Islam (orcid.org/0000-0001-7834-4100) in a new window")ab and 

Gajanan
Bhat
[![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)](http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4485-7586 "Select to open ORCID record for Gajanan Bhat (orcid.org/0000-0003-4485-7586) in a new window")\*a
  
aDepartment of Textiles, Merchandising and Interiors, University of Georgia, Athens, GA, USA. E-mail: [si59291@uga.edu](mailto:si59291@uga.edu); [gbhat@uga.edu](mailto:gbhat@uga.edu)
  
bDepartment of Textile Engineering, Dhaka University of Engineering and Technology, Gazipur 1700, Bangladesh. E-mail: [shafiqul.islam@duet.ac.bd](mailto:shafiqul.islam@duet.ac.bd)

![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)
![ORCID logo](/content/NewImages/orcid_16x16.png)

First published on 2nd February 2021

## Abstract

Synthetic materials used in a wide range of applications are prone to damage in the form of cracking/microcracking. Catastrophic failure of the materials may occur due to the growth and merging of the microcracks, which results in the reduction of the service life of the materials. To avoid these problems and increase the service life, early detection and mending of the microcracks are extremely important. Self-healing materials can be utilized in these cases, which have the capability not only to detect cracks early but also to repair cracks automatically. These materials will also have the potential to improve material reliability, extend the service life, reduce replacement costs, and improve product safety. Due to these attractive features, numerous research studies are conducted every year on the development of self-healing systems. This paper summarizes the latest progress in the design and fabrication techniques of self-healing materials through a wide range of materials, including metals, ceramics, concrete, and polymer composites. Based on recent research, this article provides an overview of different chemistries and approaches involved in preparing self-healing composites. Comparative healing efficiency and related fabrication methods are tabulated. Finally, existing problems, gaps, and challenges, and future research directions and opportunities for commercial applications are highlighted.

He pursued his undergraduate and master’s degrees in textile engineering from the Bangladesh University of Textiles. After completing the undergraduate degree, Shafiqul worked in research and developed sections of an export oriented industry. Later he joined at Dhaka University of Engineering and Technology as a faculty member. Currently, he is on study leave and pursuing his PhD degree at UGA. His main research focus is on the design and development of composite materials and production of environmentally friendly, sustainable insulation materials. He is also working on lifecycle assessment (LCA) to check the true sustainability of produced composite materials and comparing them with those of commercially available products. Another desirable property of such composites is that of self-repairing. At present, Shafiqul is investigating the possibility of incorporating the right ingredients to make self-healing composite materials.* |

![image file: d0ma00873g-p1.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-p1.gif)

Shafiqul Islam

Shafiqul Islam is a PhD student and researcher at the department of TMI of the University of Georgia. He pursued his undergraduate and master’s degrees in textile engineering from the Bangladesh University of Textiles. After completing the undergraduate degree, Shafiqul worked in research and developed sections of an export oriented industry. Later he joined at Dhaka University of Engineering and Technology as a faculty member. Currently, he is on study leave and pursuing his PhD degree at UGA. His main research focus is on the design and development of composite materials and production of environmentally friendly, sustainable insulation materials. He is also working on lifecycle assessment (LCA) to check the true sustainability of produced composite materials and comparing them with those of commercially available products. Another desirable property of such composites is that of self-repairing. At present, Shafiqul is investigating the possibility of incorporating the right ingredients to make self-healing composite materials.

Gajanan Bhat

Gajanan Bhat is the Georgia athletic association professor of fibers and textiles and department head of Textiles, Merchandising and Interiors of the University of Georgia. After earning his PhD from Georgia Tech in textile and polymer engineering, Dr Bhat joined the University of Tennessee, Knoxville (UTK) in August 1990, where his research covered nonwovens – melt blown, spunbonded and biodegradable, plastics recycling, nanotechnology, sustainable materials, and high performance fibers. Recently he joined University of Georgia as the Head of the TMI department. He has served as the president of the Fiber Society and is also an active member of INDA, TAPPI and the Textile Institute. His current research projects include: Recycling of Textile Waste into Value-added Products; Cotton-based nonwovens and composites; Stretchable cotton nonwovens; Biodegradable/Compostable Meltbown Nonwovens for Filter media and other Applications; Flushable Nonwovens; LightWeight Ballistic Materials with Improved Performance; Processing and Characterization of Carbon Fibers from Alternative Precursors.

## 1. Introduction

These different types of materials have their own self-healing mechanisms which can be categorized into several groups, including intrinsic and extrinsic self-healing approaches.[17](#cit17 "Select to navigate to references") Intrinsic self-healing normally occurs by reversible covalent bond formation with supramolecular and physical interactions of polymers.[3](#cit3 "Select to navigate to references") On the other hand, extrinsic self-healing occurs typically with the help of healing agents, which are usually stored in reservoirs like capsules,[18](#cit18 "Select to navigate to references") hollow fibers,[19,20](#cit19 "Select to navigate to reference") or vascular networks,[21,22](#cit21 "Select to navigate to reference") which are inserted in polymers or other suitable materials. When cracks occur within the materials, the capsules or vascular networks also rupture, releasing the healing agent that will close the gap and repair the material.[23](#cit23 "Select to navigate to references") Similar to the repairing process of fractured bones, three steps are involved in the extrinsic healing process.[24](#cit24 "Select to navigate to references") The first step is the triggering action that occurs soon after the damage/crack happens, the second step is the transportation of the healing agent to the damage/crack area, and the third step is the chemical interactions that heal the polymer materials.[25](#cit25 "Select to navigate to references") In both intrinsic and extrinsic self-healing, one step that is required is the initiation of self-healing in response to a stimulus/trigger.[26](#cit26 "Select to navigate to references") Along with the damage/crack, which is the main stimulus for autonomous healing, there are several other external stimuli, including optical,[4,5,27,28](#cit4 "Select to navigate to reference") thermal,[6,29](#cit6 "Select to navigate to reference") electrical,[30](#cit30 "Select to navigate to references") ballistic,[31](#cit31 "Select to navigate to references") or chemical,[32](#cit32 "Select to navigate to references") which can initiate a self-healing process. In all cases, microcracks in the materials will be repaired before propagation and catastrophic failure, resulting in increased service life of the materials.

The potential for reducing catastrophic failure and increasing the service life in self-healing materials has drawn huge attention from researchers. Every year, a significant amount of research is reported on the development of self-healing materials and use of these techniques in practical applications. The number of published papers on the topic of self-healing materials has been growing rapidly ([Fig. 1](#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure")).

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d0ma00873g-f1.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f1_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 1**  No. of published (a) papers and (b) patents from 2011 to August 2020 (according to google scholar, searched by self-healing in the title of the article). |  |

![image file: d0ma00873g-f1.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f1.gif)

From the data shown in [Fig. 1](#imgfig1 "Select to navigate to figure"), it is clear that the number of publications has been increasing rapidly over the last decade. Hence a comprehensive review article regarding self-healing materials is essential. To the best of our knowledge, previous reviews mainly focus on a general process of producing self-healing materials conducted by several researchers. A few of them focus on comparison of self-healing performance conducted by the researchers. Again, as the number of publications is increasing rapidly, a new comprehensive review paper is essential to summarize the recent advances. Hence the main objectives of this review paper are to summarize recent progresses and current challenges in self-healing materials and compare the healing performance achieved by different materials.

This paper summarizes the design and fabrication techniques of self-healing materials through a wide range of material classes, including metals, ceramics, concrete, and polymer composites. Based on recent research, this article provides an overview of different chemistries and approaches involved in the preparation of self-healing polymer composites. Comparative healing efficiencies obtained by researchers and related fabrication methods involved to obtain those efficiencies are tabulated. Finally, existing problems, gaps, and challenges of the present research are identified. The future direction of research and opportunities for commercial applications are also highlighted.

## 2. Approaches of self-healing

## 3. Intrinsic self-healing

Normally, healing functionalities of intrinsic self-healing materials remain in latent conditions in the polymeric structure. These latent functionalities are triggered by damage and driven by surface tension and elastic energy of the stress source or by an external stimulus such as heat, light, solvent, and others.[35](#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") The initiation of self-healing also relies on several other factors, including chain mobility and entanglement, reversible polymerizations, melting of thermoplastic phases, hydrogen bonding, or ionic interactions.[17](#cit17 "Select to navigate to references")

### 3.1. Chemical interactions

![[double bond, length as m-dash]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_e001.gif)
![[double bond, length as m-dash]](https://www.rsc.org/images/entities/char_e001.gif)

Several types of reversible covalent bonds like cycloaddition reactions, exchange reactions, and transesterification reactions can be utilized for self-healing purposes.

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d0ma00873g-f2.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f2_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 2**  Diels–Alder reaction. |  |

![image file: d0ma00873g-f2.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f2.gif)

A number of self-healing materials like epoxies, polyacrylates, and polyamides can be developed utilizing DA reactions.[46](#cit46 "Select to navigate to references") At elevated temperatures, the bond between diene and dienophile breaks. After that lowering, the temperature causes the bonds to reform and heal the cracks.[47](#cit47 "Select to navigate to references") Fortunato et al. obtained reversible networks by reacting a series of linear copolymers of furfuryl methacrylate with aliphatic bismaleimides through the DA reaction between furan and maleimide moieties and developed highly transparent and colorless self-healing polyacrylate coatings.[48](#cit48 "Select to navigate to references")

One of the drawbacks of DA reactions is that it requires higher temperatures to activate the self-repair process.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") The activation temperature of the retro-DA reaction is above its glass transition temperature (100 °C).[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references")

In order to reduce the healing temperature, researchers employed photochemical cycloadditions ([2+2] cycloaddition of 1,1,1-tris-(cinnamoyloxy-methyl)ethane (TCE)[49](#cit49 "Select to navigate to references") or coumarin,[50](#cit50 "Select to navigate to references") and [4+4] cycloaddition of anthracene derivatives)[27](#cit27 "Select to navigate to references") which provide an optically induced self-healing process at lower temperature.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") Self-healing chemistry of these materials is similar to DA reactions where light at different wavelengths is utilized for ring opening and closing the bond within a polymer network ([Fig. 3](#imgfig3 "Select to navigate to figure")).[49](#cit49 "Select to navigate to references") For example, polymerization of 1,1,1-tris-(cinnamoyloxymethyl)ethane (TCE) monomers via photo-stimulated [2+2] cycloaddition gives a rigid polymer.[49](#cit49 "Select to navigate to references") Coumarin [2+2] is photo-dimerized at 350 nm, and the reaction is reversed at 254 nm.[50](#cit50 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d0ma00873g-f3.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f3_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 3**  Schematic [2+2] cycloaddition upon light exposure [reproduced from ref. [35](#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier]. |  |

![image file: d0ma00873g-f3.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f3.gif)

Cycloaddition reactions have been extensively investigated by the researchers due to their good adaptability to various polymers and obtaining good self-healing properties.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") Many of the most commonly used reactions based on several recent research studies are summarized in [Table 1](#tab1 "Select to navigate to table").

| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| 41.6 MPa (shear strength) |
| Recyclable polyurethane nanocomposites | 90 °C for 5 min, then 65 °C for 48 h | 40.07 ± 2.86 MPa*[a](#tab1fna)* | >90% | [64](#cit64 "Select to navigate to references") |
| Recyclable polyurethane composites | NIR laser for 30 s, 120 °C for 2 h, then 80 °C for 24 h. | 24 MPa*[a](#tab1fna)* | 93.1% | [65](#cit65 "Select to navigate to references") |

| Cinnamoyl mechanophore | 48 h UV irradiation | — | 24% | [69](#cit69 "Select to navigate to references") |
| Dihydroxyl coumarin based polyurethane | 254 nm UV then 350 nm UV, >2 h | 3.5 MPa | 64.4% (tensile strength) | [50](#cit50 "Select to navigate to references") |

| Disulfide (S–S) exchange reactions | Polyurethane nanocomposite (polyurethane and carbon nanotubes) | 80 °C for 24 h, Irradiation of NIR laser for 1 min | 2.99 MPa*[a](#tab1fna)* | 90.1% | [70](#cit70 "Select to navigate to references") |

| Polydimethylsiloxane–polyurethane | 120 °C, 12 h | 3.31 MPa*[a](#tab1fna)* | 97% | [73](#cit73 "Select to navigate to references") |

|  | | | | | |

[54](#cit54 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from the ACS]. |  |

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d0ma00873g-f5.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f5_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 5**  Recovery of strength (%) of healed samples with different concentrations of disulfide groups [reproduced from ref. [55](#cit55 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from the ACS]. |  |

![image file: d0ma00873g-f5.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f5.gif)

Similar to disulfide bonds, thiol–disulfide ([Fig. 4(b)](#imgfig4 "Select to navigate to figure")) and silicone-based ([Fig. 4(c)](#imgfig4 "Select to navigate to figure")) exchange reactions also give self-healing properties. Tsarevsky and Matyjaszewski presented thiol–disulfide reactions based on a polystyrene copolymer, which provides good healing properties through subsequent oxidation of the thiols regenerating the disulfide linkers.[56](#cit56 "Select to navigate to references") Zheng and McCarthy showed the self-healing mechanism of crosslinked silicone-based polymers containing ethylene bridges and active silanolate end groups synthesized from tetramethylammonium silanolate-initiated ring-opening copolymerization of octamethylcyclotetrasiloxane (D4) and bis(heptamethyl cyclotetrasiloxanyl)ethane (bis-D4).[57](#cit57 "Select to navigate to references") Several of the exchange-based self-healing materials with self-healing conditions and efficiency are listed in [Table 1](#tab1 "Select to navigate to table").

In some cases, exchange reactions occur through free radical intermediates.[42](#cit42 "Select to navigate to references") When cracks or damage occur in polymer matrix, free radicals are generated due to the splitting of covalent bonds.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") Healing occurs by restoring covalent linkages when recoupling of free radicals occurs in opposite fractured surfaces.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references") Higaki et al. first incorporated thermoreversible alkoxyamine reaction into a crosslinked reversible network where crack healing occurs when free radicals are generated after cracking.[58](#cit58 "Select to navigate to references") The self-healing capacity of free-radical based materials depends on the mobility of free-radicals and avoidance of radical–oxygen interactions.[42](#cit42 "Select to navigate to references") For effective self-healing, split chain ends with reactive groups have to move to contact each other and complete all the reactions before other reactions, like radical–oxygen interactions, and capture of free radicals.[42,59](#cit42 "Select to navigate to reference") Healing of the damaged part will not occur if the free radicals interact with oxygen instead of interacting with the broken end.[60](#cit60 "Select to navigate to references")

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [image file: d0ma00873g-f6.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f6_hi-res.gif "Select to open image in new window") | | |
|  | **Fig. 6**  Hydroxy and ester groups undergo transesterification reactions [reproduced from ref. [35](#cit35 "Select to navigate to references") with permission from Elsevier]. |  |

![image file: d0ma00873g-f6.tif](/image/article/2021/MA/d0ma00873g/d0ma00873g-f6.gif)

The self-healing of supramolecular materials are generally obtained by the formation of non-covalent interactions that include hydrogen bonds, metal coordination, π–π stacking, ionic interactions, and host–guest interactions.[2,84,85](#cit2 "Select to navigate to reference") Upon applying mechanical force, the weaker supramolecular bonds dissociate, and then due to their dynamic character of this chemistry, subsequent regeneration and self-healing take place.[2](#cit2 "Select to navigate to references")

enriched_points.expanded_content: Scalability challenges in self-healing polymers arise from complex material properties, production costs, and the need for consistent healing performance across various applications.
Future research should focus on developing novel healing mechanisms, optimizing material formulations, and enhancing integration techniques for practical applications in diverse industries.
